mission.title: Install and Configure Cdk For Kubernetes Cdk8s- on Kubernetes
mission.description: cdk8s is an open-source software development framework that allows you to define Kubernetes applications and abstractions using familiar programming languages and rich object-oriented APIs. Installing cdk8s enables you to synthesize Kubernetes manifests that can be applied to any Kubernetes cluster.

steps.title: Install Node.js and npm
steps.description:
cdk8s requires Node.js and npm. Install them using the following commands:

```bash
# For Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y nodejs npm

# For macOS using Homebrew
brew install node
```

steps.title: Install cdk8s CLI globally
steps.description:
Use npm to install the cdk8s command-line interface globally:

```bash
npm install -g cdk8s-cli@0.0.0
```

steps.title: Create a new cdk8s project
steps.description:
Create a new directory for your cdk8s project and initialize it:

```bash
mkdir my-cdk8s-app
cd my-cdk8s-app
cdk8s init --language=typescript
```

steps.title: Synthesize Kubernetes manifests
steps.description:
After defining your Kubernetes resources in the `main.ts` file, synthesize the Kubernetes manifests:

```bash
cdk8s synth
```

steps.title: Apply the synthesized manifests to your Kubernetes cluster
steps.description:
Use kubectl to apply the synthesized manifests to your Kubernetes cluster:

```bash
kubectl apply -f dist/my-cdk8s-app.k8s.yaml --namespace my-namespace --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the resources are running in your Kubernetes cluster:

```bash
kubectl get all -n my-namespace
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of cdk8s will allow you to synthesize Kubernetes manifests and apply them to your cluster. You can verify the installation by checking the resources created in your specified namespace.
codeSnippets:
# For Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y nodejs npm

# For macOS using Homebrew
brew install node

mkdir my-cdk8s-app
cd my-cdk8s-app
cdk8s init --language=typescript

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the cdk8s deployment, use the following command to uninstall the release from your Kubernetes cluster. Replace 'my-cdk8s-app' with your actual release name if different.

```bash
helm uninstall my-cdk8s-app --namespace my-namespace
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any custom resource definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes that may have been created during the installation. Use the following commands:

```bash
kubectl delete crd <your-crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace my-namespace
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace used for the cdk8s application and verify that it has been removed. Use the following commands:

```bash
kubectl delete namespace my-namespace
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up your current resources. You can do this by exporting the current state of your Kubernetes resources:

```bash
kubectl get all --namespace my-namespace -o yaml > backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the cdk8s CLI to the latest version and run the upgrade command. Ensure you are using the correct version:

```bash
npm install -g cdk8s-cli@latest
helm upgrade my-cdk8s-app --namespace my-namespace
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, check the status of your resources to ensure everything is running smoothly:

```bash
kubectl get all -n my-namespace
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs to diagnose the issue. Use the following command to view the logs:

```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n my-namespace
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource not found error
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a 'Resource not found' error when applying manifests, ensure that the resource definitions are correct and that you are targeting the right namespace:

```bash
kubectl apply -f dist/my-cdk8s-app.k8s.yaml --namespace my-namespace
```

troubleshooting.title: Permission denied errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive permission denied errors, check your Kubernetes RBAC settings and ensure that your service account has the necessary permissions:

```bash
kubectl get clusterrolebindings
```

troubleshooting.title: Failed to connect to the server
troubleshooting.description:
If you see a 'Failed to connect to the server' error, verify that your Kubernetes cluster is running and that your kubeconfig is correctly configured:

```bash
kubectl cluster-info
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl configured to interact with it.
